version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes a regression in perf record that causes it to abort recording when using Zstd compression, producing an undecompressable perf.data file. The patches address three issues: a 'Bad address' error, an output-buffer overrun, and a broken zstd-error fallback. The approach taken is to fix the bugs in the compressor function, specifically in how it handles multiple compressed records and buffer sizes.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] perf record: Don't store raw data on zstd compression failure

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch prevents the perf record tool from storing raw data when ZSTD compression fails, which would lead to incorrect results and potential buffer overflows. Instead of copying the uncompressed input into the output buffer, it propagates the error. This fix addresses a problem introduced by a previous patch that added support for Zstd streaming-based compression.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Zstd compression API used by perf record, where the output buffer could be overrun when compressing data. The fix involves passing the remaining space in the output buffer to a new function process_comp_header(), which checks if the record header and padding would fit before writing them. If not, it returns -1 to stop compressing. This prevents the API from writing past the end of the output buffer, fixing an underflow bug introduced by a previous patch.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in perf record where multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records are written to the file when the compressed output exceeds the maximum record size, leading to mis-framed data that cannot be decompressed. The fix involves changing how the header is set for each record, ensuring that it accurately reflects the compressed data size and aligns properly with 8-byte boundaries. This change also reduces the maximum record size by the size of a u64 to prevent overflows in the header.size field.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/2] syscall_user_dispatch: add build/runtime configs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/2] syscall_user_dispatch: Make it configurable in Kconfig
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new Kconfig option, CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH, to make the Syscall User Dispatch feature configurable. The feature allows a thread's system calls to be intercepted and redirected to a userspace signal handler based on a prctl() configured instruction pointer range. The patch also updates various kernel files to use the new configuration option instead of relying on the existing CONFIG_GENERIC_SYSCALL setting. This change makes it possible for users to disable Syscall User Dispatch independently, rather than having it built-in under the generic syscall configuration.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/2] syscall_user_dispatch: add kernel.syscall_user_dispatch sysctl
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysctl (kernel.syscall_user_dispatch) to control whether userspace can arm syscall user dispatch via prctl or ptrace. The default value is 1, allowing arming of syscall user dispatch. Disabling the feature while it's already armed does not immediately deactivate it, but instead requires the user to attempt re-enabling via prctl or ptrace, which will fail if attempted again. This approach prevents undefined behavior in userland when translating non-Linux syscalls to Linux syscalls.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new configuration option, CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH, to make the Syscall User Dispatch feature configurable at both build and runtime. The feature is enabled by default at runtime, but can be disabled using prctl or ptrace. If a task has already armed the feature when it's disabled, it will remain active until the user attempts to re-enable it.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/5] sunrpc: route to a populated pool in svc_pool_for_cpu()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `svc_pool_for_cpu()` function in the sunrpc module to route incoming RPC requests to a populated pool, rather than one that is currently idle. This ensures that all incoming requests are serviced, even if some pools have no threads assigned to them. The change trades off NUMA locality for guaranteed service of incoming requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/5] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch hardcodes the pool mode to pernode, removing other modes from the sunrpc code. The default behavior on multi-NUMA hosts changes to one pool per NUMA node, which is considered the right choice everywhere. This change simplifies the code by removing mode-based switch statements and ensures proper thread affinity and NUMA-local memory allocation. The patch also updates documentation and preserves backward compatibility for module parameters and netlink interfaces.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/5] sunrpc: guarantee a thread per pool when auto-distributing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that when distributing threads across multiple pools in the sunrpc service, no pool is left empty even if the total number of threads requested is less than the number of pools. This is achieved by guaranteeing at least one thread per pool, which may result in a total number of threads exceeding the requested count. The change only affects the auto-distribution path and does not impact explicit per-pool thread counts set via svc_set_pool_threads().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/5] sunrpc: tear down pool counters before dropping the pool map reference
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a potential memory leak in the sunrpc service by reordering the destruction of pool counters and the dropping of the pool map reference. The original order caused issues when the last pooled service was destroyed, as it would skip destroying the percpu counters. The patch ensures that the pool counters are destroyed while the pool map is still referenced, preventing a memory leak.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/5] sunrpc: derive the pool count instead of caching it in sv_nrpools
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the cached field sv_nrpools from svc_serv and instead derives the pool count using a new helper function svc_serv_nrpools(). The function uses the existing fields sv_is_pooled and svc_pool_map.npools to calculate the correct number of pools. This change is made possible by the fact that the pool mode is always per-node, making it safe to read npools without holding the mutex. The patch also removes a modulus operation from svc_pool_for_cpu(), which should improve performance.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series hardcodes the pool mode to per-node, removing other modes and addressing issues such as scheduling RPCs to pools with no threads. The approach taken is to ensure that each populated node has at least one thread and prevent routing requests to empty pools.

review_comments.summary: Reviewer Laurent Pinchart suggested replacing the Assisted-by tag with a more concise format that includes only the LLM usage, such as 'Assisted-by: LLM # generate some test cases', and proposed rewriting the Attribution section of Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst to emphasize the importance of documenting how generative AI was used. He also expressed support for removing the agent name and model from the tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Jul 03, 2026 at 02:12:40PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> On Fri, Jul 03, 2026 at 09:05:58AM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:

> > >>>
> > >>> ISTM asking people to disclose LLM usage in a cover letter would give
> > >>> everyone what they want: Information about whether and possibly how an
> > >>> LLM was used, and it also wouldn't clutter up the changelogs with these
> > >>> tags.
> > >>
> > >> It's much much clearer and easier to just have a standardised tag for that.
> > >>
> > >> You can see that (and grep for that) immediately, vague paragraphs not so much.
> > >>
> > >
> > > At the risk of being pedantic on a point where I think the document is
> > > kind of lacking:
> > >
> > > What level of assistance crosses the bar for an "Assisted-by: LLM" tag?
> > >
> > > Some sample levels of assistance to illustrate the point:
> > >
> > > 1. I used an llm to one-shot vibe-code a patch
> > > 2. I used an llm to write a patch but carefully reviewed every line
> > > 3. I used an llm to explore the design space for a patch but wrote it
> > > manually
> > > 4. I used an llm to debug or reproduce a kernel issue but then wrote the
> > > fix manually after fully understanding the defect
> > > 5. I used an llm to review a patch I wrote
> > > 6. I used an llm to research some chunk of code while writing a patch
> > > 7. I used Google while writing a patch and learned something valuable
> > > from the AI overview at the top
> > >
> > > I personally would 100% use the tag for 1 or 2, and have already done
> > > so. I have not been doing it for 3-5, as I think that will basically
> > > make every patch llm-assisted to the point of the distinction being
> > > meaningless. If we should be doing it for 3-5 (or some subset thereof)
> > > then my mistake and I will certainly start doing so. I would hope most
> > > people agree 6-7 and similar need no tag.
> 
> I personally think 1-2 are the only relevant cases.
> 
> > >
> > > Similar questions abound if you use an llm to help with writing the
> > > English text in the patch or emails.
> > >
> > > I have a feeling that this ambiguity is part of the reason we aren't all
> > > agreeing on the value of the tag?
> >
> > Yes, I raised something similar as reply to Christian's RFC [1], where I said
> > that for me the information *how* it was used is much more important:
> >
> > "
> > Assisted-by: LLM # translate commit message
> > Assisted-by: LLM # generate some test cases
> > Assisted-by: LLM # cleanup logic
> > Assisted-by: LLM # everything and I have no clue what any in here does
> > "
> 
> Yup, and we don't need complicated rules for that just 'document what you used
> it for and give a sense of how much'.
> 
> It's fuzzy but useful.
> 
> >
> > That tag is it stands is pretty useless, really.
> 
> Not to go over it all again but I disagree, even as it stands, it allows us to
> engage in conversation about the LLM usage if admitted, and to point those who

>

> 
> But I agree with you it'd be MUCH more useful if we did the above.
> 
> I wonder if we could get consensus on adding a section to the doc saying that

> concept with some examples?

I'd support a patch that replaces

Assisted-by: Claude:claude-3-opus coccinelle sparse

with

Assisted-by: LLM # generate some test cases

and rewrites the Attribution section of
Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst accordingly.

I think most people in this mail thread have expressed that how
generative AI was used is the most important information, and several
people (including myself) have expressed a desire to stop the free
advertising. Unless I missed something, I don't think anyone has
expressed an interest in keeping the agent name and model.

> I can't imagine anybody would disagree with that, and that would get us positive
> forward progress.
> 
> Then later we could debate the details further?
> 
> > I assume most people only really use it for something in-between 1 and 2, but

> >
> > [1] https://lore.kernel.org/r/5e7b9d23-4291-48fb-bdc6-47db82d33c80@kernel.org

-- 
Regards,

Laurent Pinchart

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary: Lorenzo Stoakes expressed concerns that Jeff Layton's argument for removing LLM attribution requirements is flawed, as it only considers the lack of universal adherence to the rule and not its actual usage or value in facilitating engagement and data collection. He suggested a compromise where the requirement would be simplified to include only 'LLM' and a comment on the extent of AI-generated code, with an additional paragraph for subsystem-specific acceptance.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm a little surprised I'm cc'd on this :) I'm not entirely sure if my pushing
back on this is going to mean anything but I suppose here goes nothing.

On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 10:32:48AM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> We've had this requirement in place in the Documentation for several
> months, but it's becoming clear that the signal to noise ratio from this
> is quite low.
>
> 1/ It's not universally followed. While many people do try to attribute
> the LLMs in good faith, not everyone does for various reasons.

Does something not being universally followed therefore make it worthless?

You really have to explain that, because this is literally true of any rule
whatsoever we might have in the kernel, should we drop all of them?

I think you should replace this with a cogent argument such as that you feel it
is not being used in the _majority_ of cases or is very rarely used, and that
value is in your view not there.

>
> 2/ It basically serves as free advertising for proprietary LLM companies.

I agree with this point, we should drop the model.

>
> 3/ It's not clear why we want to collect this info in the first place.

Well I made arguments on the other thread, but to repeat:

- It makes it easier to engage with people when they do ack it.

- It makes it far quicker to be able to do so.

- There's a barrier to mentioning an LLM if it's not provided - people can get
  upset, or it can cause issues to raise it as a concern.

- Even if it's only there in _some_ cases, it makes _those_ cases easier to deal
  with.

- It provides some (incomplete) data that might make it easier to deal with
  bug-causing patches.

- It provides some (incomplete) data on bug rates with/without LLMs.

I do agree they're far far less useful when there's not some indication of how
much of the patch was LLM-generated.

Their usefulness is obviously deeply far from perfect, but not zero.

>
> Given that the data this provides is flawed at best and is being
> collected for a purpose that isn't clear, let's just kill the
> requirement for these tags from the kernel at large.

I feel there are purposes. Perhaps the argument is stronger for having the tags
on submissions rather than actually in-tree, however.

>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

I think simplifying by requiring only 'LLM' and adding a line simply suggesting
a comment giving a sense of how much was used, and then perhaps a paragraph saying

	Subsystems may vary in their acceptance of this patch blah blah blah

Could be a compromise?

> ---
> Christian had proposed watering down the LLM attribution, but I think
> it's not productive to try and track this until we have a clearer sense
> of what we want to do with this information and how to make it more
> reliable.

Well he's moved now to stridently saying we should do what you're doing here so
presumably you're now in alignment.

What level of assistance crosses the bar for an "Assisted-by: LLM" tag?

Some sample levels of assistance to illustrate the point:

3. I used an llm to explore the design space for a patch but wrote it
manually

fix manually after fully understanding the defect
5. I used an llm to review a patch I wrote

so. I have not been doing it for 3-5, as I think that will basically

meaningless. If we should be doing it for 3-5 (or some subset thereof)

review_comments.author: Theodore Tso
review_comments.summary: Theodore Tso noted that his workflow involves asking an LLM to modify code, but then manually reviewing and testing it before writing the commit description himself, which differs from the scenario where an LLM generates thousands of lines of code for a pull request. He emphasized that there's a significant distinction between modifying existing code with an LLM and 'vibe coding' a new OS from scratch.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 12:46:19PM -0500, Andreas Dilger wrote:
> It would make sense IMHO to address the ignorance in the most expedient manner,
> namely by telling the *LLM itself* to add this tag when it generates the commit
> message and/or reviews the code.
> 
> This could be achieved by adding a statement in "AGENTS.md" in the root of the
> source tree to this effect, or telling the agent to reference and follow rules
> in Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst regarding the Assisted-by: tag.

This presupposes a particular workflow where the developer allows the
LLM to generate git commits.  I don't do that.  I'll ask the LLM to
modify the code, but then I'll generally fix it up --- very often by
hand because I can edit the sources than entering a prompt and waiting
for the LLM to figure out how to make the change.  I'll often run the
test cases myself, since if it takes 24 hours of VM time, and 2 hours
of wall clock time, I'm going to kick off the regression tests myself,
and verify the test results.

And I almost always write the commit description myself.  So when I
say, "Assisted-by" it literally is "Assisted by".  It is not "vibe
coded where the LLM generates thousands of lines of code that the
human being doesn't understand before sending ta pull request."  And
that's probably why you'll see people asserting that LLM generated
code can't possibly be accepted due to copyright reasons.  There's a
big difference between asking an LLM to modify already existing code
to add a feature, or to fix a bug, and to vibe code a new OS from
scratch.  One is far less likely to be a copyright violation than the
other.

						- Ted

review_comments.author: Mauro Chehab
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Mauro Chehab noted that the Assisted-by tag for LLMs does not provide useful information, as it is unclear whether the patch was generated by a specific model or tool, and suggested instead requiring patches to be written or reviewed by humans. He also proposed adding a warning in Sashiko's review process to identify AI-generated patches.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 3 Jul 2026 07:42:35 -0400
"Theodore Tso" <tytso@mit.edu> wrote:

> On Fri, Jul 03, 2026 at 09:30:28AM -0500, Greg KH wrote:
> > > So clarifying there that a "Assisted-by: LLM" is also good enough would make
> > > sense if we agree on that.  
> > 
> > If we all agree on that, I'm saying that _I_ don't care, but others
> > might.  
> 
> I agree that requiring the model name is not a good idea, because some
> developers might be using unreleased products where the name itself
> might not be public.  So allowing the version number of the LLM to be
> elided would be a good change.
> 
> I'll also note that it's starting to be the case where tool is
> actually quite relevant.  So it's not just about the LLM Model, but
> whether you're using Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, or Shahiko.  You
> can use the tool or the harness with different models.
> 
> So whether the tag is:
> 
> Assisted-By: OpenCode:Gemini 3.5 Pro
> Assisted-By: Claude Code
> Assisted-By: LLM

I don't think an assisted-by for LLM actually helps.

I can't see any difference on adding it or doing something like:

	Assisted-By: smath
	Assisted-By: sparse
	Assisted-By: coverity
	Assisted-By: gcc
	...
	Assisted-By: my-favorite-web-search-engine

From my side, I don't care about whatever tool/process a developer has
to produce a patch. All I want is a proper patch that passes my review
criteria.

Also, identifying bad vibe-coding patches is usually easy: their 
descriptions look like written by a former literature professor that just 
took his first job as junior programmer: they come with useless comments,
random variable renames, unneeded code rewrites, etc.

Btw, if one wants them to be tagged as AI-produced, perhaps we can teach 
Sashiko to identify it and add a big warning on its review. This will
probably be more reliable than asking people to add a LLM tag.

What I think we should do, instead, is to let it clear that vibe coding
is forbidden - e.g. just asking AI to do something and sending it as-is
(*).
So, I would add something like this at
Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst:

	"All patches shall be written or reviewed and modified by a human.
	 You should be able to understand exactly what the patch is doing,
	 why it is taking such approach and how. Pure non-trivial
	 machine-generated patches will be rejected."

(*) There are of course exceptions for it: typo fix patches for instance
    can be pure machine-generated. Even before AI, we have patches like
    that where people used some spell checker to fix typos. Again, they
    need human review before submission, as sometimes, spell checker fails
    when, for instance, it tries to replace an acronym with resembles
    a word.

-- 
Thanks,
Mauro

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 01/18] iomap: add ->iomap_next() and iomap_process() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new callback function called ->iomap_next() that replaces the existing ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() functions. The ->iomap_next() function is responsible for finishing the previous mapping if needed and producing the next mapping, allowing performance-critical callers to inline the iteration with a fixed callback. A helper function called iomap_process() is also added to handle most of the logic required in an ->iomap_next() implementation. This change allows filesystems to be converted one at a time using the new ->iomap_next() function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 02/18] xfs: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the iomap operations for XFS to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is a more efficient and flexible way of handling I/O mappings. The conversion uses the iomap_process() helper function to finish previous mappings and produce the next one. This change does not introduce any functional changes but rather updates the internal implementation of XFS's iomap operations. The patch affects several files in the fs/xfs directory, including xfs_file.c and xfs_iomap.c.

review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested bikeshedding the name of the ->iomap_next() function, questioning its purpose and whether it should be named after looking up the first mapping, releasing resources to look for the next mapping, or deciding when to stop iterating.

Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested renaming xfs_direct_write_iomap_next() to xfs_direct_write_iomap_iter(), citing that it would better describe the function's purpose of iterating iomaps for direct writes.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the introduction of 'iomap_iter' creates a naming conflict and suggested renaming the iomap_process function to something like 'iomap_iter_yield', but acknowledged the difficulty in choosing a suitable name due to C's lack of Python-like naming conventions.
review_comments.raw_body:
<unvarnished brainstream ahead>

Now that I see the callsites, I think the "next" name could use some
bikeshedding <cough>.  The purpose of this function is either

(a) to look up the first mapping to start iterating;
(b) to release whatever resources were attached during the current
    iteration and look up the next mapping to continue iterating; or
(c) to decide that it's time to stop iterating.

---

iomaps for iteration.  In Python those are called generator functions;
in Rust they're called objects that implement the Iterator trait (or
iterators for short).

How about s/iomap_next/iomap_iter/ ?

Then this function would be called xfs_direct_write_iomap_iter, which
IMO is a closer description of what the function does, which is to say
iterates iomaps for direct writes.

(Yes, my brain might be polluted with thinking that "iomap next" refers
to a major shift, in the sense of "linux next". :P)

But then "iomap_iter" sets up a new problem: should iomap_process have a
new name that goes along with that?  iomap_iter is already taken for the
legacy path.  If we were emulating python I'd suggest iomap_iter_yield
but this is C so we get to reinvent everything from scratch so who
knows.

--D

#define __default_iomap_end(a,b,...) b
#define default_iomap_end(...) __default_iomap_end(,##__VA_ARGS,NULL)

#define iomap_process(iter, iomap, srcmap, iomap_begin, ...) \
	__iomap_process((iter), (iomap), (srcmap), (iomap_begin),
			default_iomap_end(__VA_ARGS__))

series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the ntfs3 file system to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is a more efficient way of handling iomap operations. series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 05/18] ntfs: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the NTFS file system's iomap operations to use the new ->iomap_next() callback. This change does not introduce any functional changes, but rather adapts the NTFS code to the new iomap API. The patch updates several functions in fs/ntfs/iomap.c to use the new iomap_next callback, including ntfs_read_iomap_ops, ntfs_zero_read_iomap_ops, and ntfs_write_iomap_ops.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 18/18] Documentation: iomap: update docs to reflect iomap_next model
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the iomap documentation to reflect the new iomap_next model, where every iomap operation takes a single iomap_next callback directly. The callback finishes the previous mapping and produces the next one. Most filesystems build it from begin and end helpers via the iomap_process helper. This change replaces the separate ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() callbacks used in older versions of iomap.

Reviewer Darrick Wong pointed out that the documentation for iomap_end should be updated to reflect that it must persist data for write operations, specifically mentioning that iomap_writepages does not handle inline data.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the documentation for iomap_next() does not accurately describe its behavior, specifically that it is responsible for finishing the previous mapping in addition to producing the next one.

Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested adding a transition sentence to clarify that filesystems should call iomap_process instead of implementing all behaviors in ->iomap_next themselves, and also proposed demoting the next section for better clarity.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the documentation update looks good and is fine as it is, indicating no issues or requested changes.

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the iomap_next model does not allow for the ->begin method to set the private field and the ->end method to dispose of it, as the next ->begin would pass a new mapping to the next ->begin. He suggested making iomap_iter_continue preserve the iomap/srcmap private pointer across memset(0) calls or changing the signature of iomap_process to avoid passing iomap/srcmap explicitly.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the documentation still uses '->iomap_begin' and '->iomap_end' callbacks in some places, which should be updated to use 'iomap_next' instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
The ->begin method can still set iomap::private and the ->end method can
dispose of it, right?  Oh, wait, no, that doesn't work because you're
talking about ->begin/->end passing something to the next ->begin.

I suppose you could make iomap_iter_continue preserve the iomap/srcmap
private pointer across the memset(0) calls.  But all current users
either rely on private being zeroed by iomap_iter after ->iomap_end
returns, so that would be quite the change.

Hm.  I was thinking that the signature for iomap_process could be
cleaner if you didn't have to pass iomap/srcmap explicitly.
iomap_process could do the (dangerous) casting from the (const struct
iomap_iter *) to the (struct iomap *) pointers before calling ->begin
and ->end.

But if you do have an ->iomap_next function that doesn't use
iomap_process, then it has to do the pointer extraction itself.  We
already expose the innards of struct iomap_iter to callers, so maybe
it's ok to have some gross helpers like:

static inline struct iomap *iomap_iter_iomap(const struct iomap_iter *i)
{
	return (struct iomap *)&i->iomap;
}

static inline struct iomap *iomap_iter_srcmap(const struct iomap_iter *i)
{
	return (struct iomap *)&i->srcmap;
}

I'm not sure *that*'s any cleaner.  And I think it gets confusing with
the other srcmap extraction function.

Eh never mind, I've talked myself out of this. ;)

---

<nod>

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that he had previously used the iomap.private field to store per-mapping private data during the fuse-iomap development work, but this approach is no longer relevant and does not constitute a strong argument against the current design.

Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested changing the signature of the iomap_begin_fn callback to take a struct iomap_iter pointer instead of passing parts of it as separate arguments.

Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested simplifying the call sites of the iomap_next callback by passing a single const struct iomap_iter pointer instead of three separate arguments, and explicitly casting to remove constness from the two iomappings.
review_comments.raw_body:
I dunno -- towards the end of the fuse-iomap development work I actually
had started using iomap.private to store per-mapping private data.  But
that work is dead now, so that's not a strong argument.

---

Oh, you mean changing the signature to:

typedef int (iomap_begin_fn)(struct iomap_iter *iter...);

instead of passing parts of the iter as separate arguments?
Yeah, that would be nice.

---

Let me try again.  Instead of passing three arguments:

int fubar_iomap_next(const struct iomap_iter *iter,
	struct iomap *i, struct iomap *s)
{
	...
}

pass one instead:

int fubar_iomap_next(const struct iomap_iter *iter)
{
	struct iomap *i = (struct iomap *)&iter->iomap;
	struct iomap *s = (struct iomap *)&iter->srcmap;
	...

to simplify the call sites.  The gross part of this is (1) having a
const iomap_iter pointer to prevent filesystems from screwing around
with the iter, and therefore (2) the need for explicit casting to remove
the constness from the two iomappings.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series converts the iomap code to use an iterator model, replacing the legacy ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() interface. The conversion is mechanical, with no functional changes intended. The new ->iomap_next() interface allows filesystems to provide direct begin/end helper functions, which are then used by the iomap_process() inline helper. The patches also update the documentation to reflect the new iterator model.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the iomap_next() callback being used instead of ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end(). They explained that ->iomap_next() finishes the previous mapping if needed, and produces the next mapping. The compiler can inline the iteration with a fixed callback, which is more efficient.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed Darrick Wong's concern that ->iomap_next() is still an indirect call at the end of the series, and explained that performance-critical callers can inline the iteration with a fixed callback which the compiler can call directly.
review_comments.raw_body:
Convert erofs iomap_ops to the new ->iomap_next() callback. This uses the
iomap_process() helper, which finishes the previous mapping if needed
and produces the next one. No functional changes are intended.

Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
---
 fs/erofs/data.c | 10 ++++++++--
 fs/erofs/zmap.c |  9 ++++++++-
 2 files changed, 16 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/erofs/data.c b/fs/erofs/data.c
index 9aa48c8d67d1..47dba61ec576 100644
--- a/fs/erofs/data.c
+++ b/fs/erofs/data.c
@@ -380,9 +380,15 @@ static int erofs_iomap_end(struct inode *inode, loff_t pos, loff_t length,
 	return written;
 }
 
+static int erofs_iomap_next(const struct iomap_iter *iter, struct iomap *iomap,
+			    struct iomap *srcmap)
+{
+	return iomap_process(iter, iomap, srcmap, erofs_iomap_begin,
+			     erofs_iomap_end);
+}
+
 static const struct iomap_ops erofs_iomap_ops = {
-	.iomap_begin = erofs_iomap_begin,
-	.iomap_end = erofs_iomap_end,
+	.iomap_next = erofs_iomap_next,
 };
 
 int erofs_fiemap(struct inode *inode, struct fiemap_extent_info *fieinfo,
diff --git a/fs/erofs/zmap.c b/fs/erofs/zmap.c
index bab521613552..dd058413a0b6 100644
--- a/fs/erofs/zmap.c
+++ b/fs/erofs/zmap.c
@@ -821,6 +821,13 @@ static int z_erofs_iomap_begin_report(struct inode *inode, loff_t offset,
 	return 0;
 }
 
+static int z_erofs_iomap_next_report(const struct iomap_iter *iter,
+				     struct iomap *iomap, struct iomap *srcmap)
+{
+	return iomap_process(iter, iomap, srcmap, z_erofs_iomap_begin_report,
+			     NULL);
+}
+
 const struct iomap_ops z_erofs_iomap_report_ops = {
-	.iomap_begin = z_erofs_iomap_begin_report,
+	.iomap_next = z_erofs_iomap_next_report,
 };
--

> > <unvarnished brainstream ahead>
> >
> > Now that I see the callsites, I think the "next" name could use some
> > bikeshedding <cough>.  The purpose of this function is either
> >
> > (a) to look up the first mapping to start iterating;
> > (b) to release whatever resources were attached during the current
> >     iteration and look up the next mapping to continue iterating; or
> > (c) to decide that it's time to stop iterating.
> >
> > From that it seems obvious to me that xfs_direct_write_iomap_next yields
> > iomaps for iteration.  In Python those are called generator functions;
> > in Rust they're called objects that implement the Iterator trait (or
> > iterators for short).
> >
> > How about s/iomap_next/iomap_iter/ ?
> >
> > Then this function would be called xfs_direct_write_iomap_iter, which
> > IMO is a closer description of what the function does, which is to say
> > iterates iomaps for direct writes.
> >
> > (Yes, my brain might be polluted with thinking that "iomap next" refers
> > to a major shift, in the sense of "linux next". :P)
>
> Thanks for sharing your thoughts. The idea makes sense to me in theory
> but I think in rust and python the "iter" naming is reserved for the
> iterator object itself and the method / callback for advancing and
> yielding the next item in the iteration is named "next" (eg
> Iterator::next in rust and __next__() in python)? I wonder if the
> s/_next/_iter gets confusing with multiple things in iomap already
> called iter (eg the struct iomap_iter, the iomap_iter() function). I'm
> not sure, I'm happy to rename this if that's the preference.
>

Ahh okay, I think I see the point you were trying to make. The
callsite functions are like:

ssize_t iomap_file_buffered_write(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *from,
                iomap_next_fn iomap_next,
                const struct iomap_write_ops *write_ops, void *private);

int iomap_fsverity_write(struct file *file, loff_t pos, size_t length,
                const void *buf, iomap_next_fn iomap_next,
                const struct iomap_write_ops *write_ops);

void iomap_read_folio(iomap_next_fn iomap_next,
                struct iomap_read_folio_ctx *ctx, void *private);

void iomap_readahead(iomap_next_fn iomap_next,
                struct iomap_read_folio_ctx *ctx, void *private);

etc, so it's not clear from the "iomap_next_fn iomap_next" naming that
it handles per-iteration logic, whereas renaming it to "iomap_iter_fn
iomap_iter" makes it more explicit. Am I interpreting this correctly?

I think this is a good idea. If no one has an objection, I'll make
this change for v3.

Thanks,
Joanne

>

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary:
Jan Kara noted that the iomap_next function pointer should be passed directly to iomap_iter() and other entry points, instead of being wrapped in a struct iomap_ops, as all filesystems have implemented ->iomap_next() and the legacy fallback is gone.

Reviewer Jan Kara noted that the patch series removes the legacy ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() path, but does not update all filesystems to pass their ->iomap_next function directly instead of an ops struct. He suggested adding Acked-by for ext2 & ext4 changes.

>

> +	 * The retry mechanism is based on the ->iomap_next method returning

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that even after converting to an in-iter iomap_next() model, performance-critical callers would still make indirect calls to ->iomap_next(), and suggested adding a comment to the __always_inline iomap_process function explaining its importance for compilers to inline these calls.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 05:09:16PM -0700, Joanne Koong wrote:
> Have one ->iomap_next() callback instead of ->iomap_begin() and
> ->iomap_end(). ->iomap_next() finishes the previous mapping if needed,
> and produces the next mapping. This lets performance-critical callers
> inline the iteration with a fixed callback, which the compiler is able
> to call directly instead of indirectly.

Er... what is being called directly here?  The iomap_{begin,end}
functions?  It looks to me like ->iomap_next is still an indirect call
even at the end of the series, right?  That's still a net reduction of
indirect calls at least.

Oh, wait, it's the __always_inline iomap_process function that a smart
compiler can use to turn the indirect calls into direct ones, isn't it?
It might be useful to add a comment to that function saying that out
loud so that dolts like me will pick up on why it's critical for it to
be an inline function.

I like how this is going. :)

> iomap_iter() uses ->iomap_next() when the filesystem provides that

You /could/ use a bunch of fugly macros to simulate a default NULL
iomap_end_fn parameter.

#define __default_iomap_end(a,b,...) b
#define default_iomap_end(...) __default_iomap_end(,##__VA_ARGS,NULL)

#define iomap_process(iter, iomap, srcmap, iomap_begin, ...) \

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary: Darrick Wong suggested renaming the iomap_next() callback to something more descriptive, such as iomap_iter(), but Joanne pointed out that in Rust and Python, 'iter' is typically reserved for the iterator object itself, and the method/callback for advancing and yielding the next item in the iteration is named 'next'. Darrick proposed using iomap_yield() or sticking with iomap_next() to avoid extra work.

returns, so that would be quite the change.

cleaner if you didn't have to pass iomap/srcmap explicitly.

and ->end.

iomap_process, then it has to do the pointer extraction itself.  We
already expose the innards of struct iomap_iter to callers, so maybe
it's ok to have some gross helpers like:

static inline struct iomap *iomap_iter_iomap(const struct iomap_iter *i)
{

}

static inline struct iomap *iomap_iter_srcmap(const struct iomap_iter *i)
{
	return (struct iomap *)&i->srcmap;
}

I'm not sure *that*'s any cleaner.  And I think it gets confusing with
the other srcmap extraction function.

Eh never mind, I've talked myself out of this. ;)

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm: mempolicy: fix automatic numa balancing for shmem

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: mempolicy: fix automatic numa balancing for shmem

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem where mapped shared memory (shmem) was not considered for NUMA balancing on tiered systems. The issue arose from a previous commit that intended to limit NUMA scanning to migrate-on-fault VMAs, but incorrectly treated the absence of a shared policy as an explicit opt-out. The patch changes the vma_policy_mof function to use __get_vma_policy directly, allowing default policy VMAs to participate in balancing while still excluding explicitly pinned memory. This fix should improve performance on tiered systems with cachelib-based workloads.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Gregory Price expressed strong disagreement (CONTENTIOUS) with the patch, stating that demotions don't care about mempolicy and that opting shmem out of NUMA balancing on a tiered system is problematic. He also emphasized that this issue highlights the need for a complete overhaul of the demotion mechanism to prevent undefined behavior in memory placement.

Gave Reviewed-by
review_comments.raw_body:
Eugh.

Demotions don't care about mempolicy, so opting shmem out of NUMA
balancing and mbind'ing on a tiered system is just full sadness.

This is all just more evidence that demotion needs to be completely
redone, it's creating a mess of undefined behavior for memory placement.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that the patch's handling of NULL from vm_ops->get_policy was incorrect, and agrees that using __get_vma_policy() directly is a better approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
Right, mbinding in tiered mode is a whole other ball of wax. I'm just

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Gregory Price noted that task-wide policies can be problematic, citing an example where a stack depot pool inherits the task's NUMA memory policy and becomes stranded on a node forever. He also highlighted the issue of alloc_pages() calls inheriting task mempolicies in various parts of the kernel, which can lead to unexpected behavior such as spreading userland data across nodes.

Reviewer noted that direct reclaim and kswapd have different cgroup considerations, making cross-cpuset checks potentially complex to implement.
review_comments.raw_body:
It can be, but there's also many footguns with task-wide policy.

Something i found while seeing if i could make ZONE_NORMAL nodes more
reliably hotpluggable:

diff --git a/lib/stackdepot.c b/lib/stackdepot.c
index dd2717ff94bf..9ceeb56574ef 100644
--- a/lib/stackdepot.c
+++ b/lib/stackdepot.c
@@ -682,7 +682,15 @@ depot_stack_handle_t stack_depot_save_flags(unsigned long *entries,
 	 * we won't be able to do that under the lock.
 	 */
 	if (unlikely(can_alloc && !READ_ONCE(new_pool))) {
-		page = alloc_pages(gfp_nested_mask(alloc_flags),
+		/*
+		 * The stack depot pool is a global, never-freed allocation.
+		 * Use alloc_pages_node() on the CPU-local node instead of
+		 * alloc_pages() so the pool does not inherit a transient task's
+		 * NUMA mempolicy (e.g. MPOL_BIND to a CPU-less/bound node), which
+		 * would strand this long-lived page on that node forever.
+		 */
+		page = alloc_pages_node(numa_node_id(),
+				   gfp_nested_mask(alloc_flags),
 				   DEPOT_POOL_ORDER);
 		if (page)
 			prealloc = page_address(page);

This is a global, permanently allocated, resource that inherits a task
mempolicy's placement because that task *happened* to be the first one
to touch it.

There are many alloc_pages() calls (155 instances kernel-wide) that
inherit a task mempolicy when that's probably not what we want.

alloc_pages() is called in: net/, lib/, kexec_core/, drivers/, arch/

you can imagine a task setting `set_mempolicy(INTERLEAVE, ALL)` and the
result is a bunch of random driver memory gets spread all over the place
along with the task's heap.  Is that really what the caller wanted, or
did they just want userland data spread about?

But at this point it's a 20 year old interface, not much we can do about
it without making *someone* sad :[

I considered proposing MPOL_F_MOVABLE_ONLY to mean (roughly) "userland
memory only" - and then slowly trying to migrate numactl to make this
the default.

---

Direct reclaim considers cpusets for the reclaiming task (added
recently), kswapd sits in its own cgroup.

Cross-cpuset checks - i'm not sure how tractable that is.  We ignore it
for now, recognizing that if something is cross-cpuset it's some
definition of shared/global object (e.g. pagecache mappings).

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that kernel allocations should follow task policy, but noted it's an annotation problem already solved by cgroups, and suggested renaming GFP_ACCOUNT flags to clarify the distinction.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hm. Kernel allocations that are totally incidental like the stackdepot
example above should not follow task policy. But there are kernel
allocations (kernel stack, inodes, pipes) that are directly allocated
on behalf of a process, and so probably SHOULD follow task policy.

That's an annotation problem that I think we have solved already,
because cgroups need the same distinction for what allocations to
charge to the current task's cgroup context.

We could rename __GFP_ACCOUNT / SLAB_ACCOUNT to __GFP_TASKPOLICY /
SLAB_TASKPOLICY or something, and have mempolicy follow it too.

There is still the whole "changing 20 year old behavior" aspect, but I
think the polarity works in our favor: big important allocations have
already been following the policy correctly. The behavior changes
primarily for smaller, random allocations.

review_comments.raw_body:
What is the right mempolicy for these random kernel allocations?  If the
pages are used privately by the task (not the case in your example, but
is it possible?), is it OK to use the mempolicy of the task?  If the
pages are shared, what is the correct mempolicy?  Will it be fixed?  Or
can we find a way to change the policy?

[snip]

---
Best Regards,
Huang, Ying

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Gregory Price noted that the patch's allocations should use the system default (local node) instead of the task policy, and suggested dropping the first commit to ship only the second one.
review_comments.raw_body:
I submitted a recommendation[1], but I think it got swalled by the kvack
outage, i'll probably need to re-send.

At the very least, these allocations should just use the system default
(local node) instead of the task.

I left the page-cache affected by this (so inode data DOES follow this)
but after some testing I found this amounts to around ~0.4% of total
memory usage when you have many small files (~64kb files, 512GB cached).

I think we can probably drop the first commit and just ship the second.

The fun result - when you combine this fix + private nodes [2] you can
reliably hotplug a private node in ZONE_NORMAL so long as all tasks that
were using the private node were reaped prior to hotplug.

This is a legitimately useful improvement in my opinion.

~Gregory

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260701222112.2820098-1-gourry@gourry.net/
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260222084842.1824063-1-gourry@gourry.net/
